data.definition: A quadratic equation is an equation which contains the square of the unknown quantity, but no higher power, or an equation of second degree.
data.origin: Originated nearly 4,000 years ago in ancient Babylon to solve practical land division and agricultural problems. Later evolved through Indian and Islamic algebraic breakthroughs before Rene Descartes formalized the modern formula and notation in 17th century Europe, transforming how we solve for unknown variables today.

explination.title: General Form
explination.content: The general form for a quadratic equation is : ax^2 + bx + c = 0, where x is the unknown quantity, a, b, c belong to real numbers and a is not equal to 0 (as it will become linear otherwise).

explination.title: Degree and Solutions
explination.content: As the degree (highest power of the unknown value) of a quadratic equation is 2, it must have at most 2 real solutions (values of the unknown quantity that satisfy the equation).

explination.title: Graph of a quadratic
explination.content: If you plot a quadratic function y = ax^2 + bx + c in a 2D plane, you will find that its graph is of parabolic shape with the following components: x-intercepts (the roots), y-intercept, a vertex (minima/maxima) and the line of symmetry. If (the coefficient of x^2) a > 0, it is an upward opening parabola; if a < 0, it is a downward opening parabola.

explination.title: Solutions
explination.content: A solution refers to the values for x that satisfy the given equation. For solving such equations there are many ways, such as factorization, completing the square, using the quadratic formula, the Po-Shen-Lo method, etc.

explination.title: Factorization - splitting the middle term
explination.content: If a quadratic equation can be expressed in the form of ax^2 + (a + b)x + b, its middle term can be split into ax + bx and then the first and last two terms can be grouped and factored to get a product of two linear terms. Eventually after solving those linear equations we will get its roots.

explination.title: Completing the square
explination.content: It is the most ancient way to solve a general quadratic equation of any pattern. The main concept includes visualizing the equation as a literal square and rectangular pieces and finding the missing portion to complete the square and find the solutions. For example, we have the equation x^2 + 14x = 32, so we attempt to make the LHS a perfect square i.e. (x + 7)^2 = 81, then taking the square root of both sides and subtracting 7 we get x = 2, -16.

explination.title: Quadratic Formula
explination.content: The most common way to solve a quadratic is using the quadratic formula given by the Indian mathematician Sridharacharya i.e. x = -b + sqrt(b^2 - 4ac) / 2a & -b - sqrt(b^2 - 4ac) / 2a, where a, b, c are the coefficients from the general form. That is basically a generalized extension of the completing the square method.

explination.title: Vieta's theorem
explination.content: It states that the sum of roots of a quadratic equation is -(coefficient of x) / coefficient of x^2 i.e. -b/a. And the product of roots is constant term / coefficient of x^2 i.e. c/a. It also extends to higher degree polynomials or equations, no matter if real roots exist or not.

explination.title: Discriminant
explination.content: It is possible that the given equation has no real solution at all. We can determine that using the discriminant of a quadratic equation. The discriminant (D) is defined as b^2 - 4ac. If D > 0, the equation has 2 real solutions; if D = 0, the equation has only one real solution; and if D < 0, the equation has no real solutions. NOTE: If the discriminant is 0, the roots are just -b/2a.

explination.title: Po-Shen-Lo method of solving
explination.content: It is a unique way of solving a quadratic. First we calculate the sum and product of roots by the given relation: sum of roots = -b/a, product of roots = c/a. And we know that the vertex of a parabola is the midpoint of the roots, thus v = sum of roots/2 or v = -b/2a. Then as the distance between both the roots and the line of symmetry is constant, let it be 'u'. Now, the roots will be v + u and v - u. Finally, using the product relation, we will form the equation: v^2 - u^2 = P, calculate the value of u and substitute it back to the roots equation and we are done.
